symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden severe non-cyclical generalised pelvic pain radiating to both thighs, a change from usual pattern

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Severe exacerbation of pelvic pain on a background of known endometriosis; bilateral/generalised distribution fits central sensitisation.
Sudden severe pelvic pain mandates active exclusion of torsion, even at low probability.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe dysmenorrhoea and known chronic pelvic pain (endometriosis)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Established endometriosis is the substrate for the flare and for the central-sensitisation phenotype.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain spikes triggered by bladder filling and defaecation (visceral hyperalgesia)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normally innocuous visceral events triggering severe pain is characteristic of central sensitisation / neurogenic amplification.
Reproducible visceral triggers over hours fit a sensitisation picture rather than an acutely torted, ischaemic adnexa.

symptoms.symptom_name: Nausea and vomiting with inability to keep fluids down

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Severe pain commonly produces reflex nausea/vomiting and autonomic upset; non-specific.
Vomiting is a common feature of torsion — one reason it cannot be excluded on symptoms alone.
Non-specific; vomiting occurs in appendicitis but here without localisation or fever.

symptoms.symptom_name: Maximal over-the-counter analgesia already failed (naproxen + paracetamol-codeine)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Poor response to standard oral analgesia is typical of central sensitisation flares and signals the oral route is exhausted.
Fatigue is common in chronic pain / central sensitisation but does not discriminate between causes.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reliable continuous hormonal contraception, no abnormal vaginal bleeding

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reliable continuous contraception and absence of abnormal bleeding lower ectopic probability — but a pregnancy test is still required to exclude it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia (heart rate ~108) and tachypnoea secondary to pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pain-driven autonomic response; a clinician-measured vital sign, sealed — the AI infers distress from the patient's report of a racing heart, not a measured value.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normotensive with no haemodynamic compromise (BP ~115/75, has not fainted)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of hypotension/shock argues against active intra-abdominal haemorrhage; a clinician-measured value, so telehealth relies on the patient not having fainted.

symptoms.symptom_name: Soft abdomen with diffuse hyperalgesia but NO rebound, guarding or rigidity

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of peritonism argues against an acute surgical abdomen — but this is a clinician examination finding, unobtainable via telehealth.
Examination is part of excluding torsion, but imaging is definitive; neither is available remotely.
No fever lowers the probability of an infective/inflammatory surgical cause; temperature and CVA tenderness are clinician-obtained.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Generalised out-of-proportion pain + visceral (bladder/bowel) triggering + failure of standard oral analgesia together point to a central sensitisation flare rather than a new acute surgical cause — but recognition of the pattern does not remove the need to exclude torsion, which is unexcludable remotely.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Mandatory in any reproductive-age woman with acute pelvic pain to exclude ectopic pregnancy, regardless of stated contraception — requires in-person/point-of-care testing.
The definitive test to exclude ovarian torsion and assess adnexal pathology; cannot be performed via telehealth — a reason for in-person referral.
Screens for urinary tract infection / renal cause contributing to pelvic pain and pain on bladder filling.
Assesses for an inflammatory/infective process (e.g. appendicitis) if the picture does not settle.
Not first-line for a reproductive-age woman with pelvic pain — ultrasound and a pregnancy test come first; routine CT would be over-investigation and unnecessary radiation.
